text_summary: Tumor Mutational Burden High (TMB-H) is defined as 10 or more somatic mutations per megabase of DNA sequenced. TMB-H is a pan-cancer predictive biomarker for response to immune checkpoint inhibitors, with pembrolizumab receiving tissue-agnostic FDA approval for TMB-H solid tumors based on the KEYNOTE-158 trial. TMB is measured by comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) using validated NGS panels such as FoundationOne CDx. TMB varies by cancer type, with melanoma, NSCLC, and bladder cancer having among the highest median values. High TMB correlates with neoantigen generation and enhanced anti-tumor immune recognition. TMB should be interpreted alongside PD-L1 expression and MSI status for optimal immunotherapy patient selection.
predictive_value: Response to immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab)
testing_method: Comprehensive genomic profiling (NGS)
clinical_cutoff: >=10 mutations/Mb

text_summary: Microsatellite Instability-High (MSI-H) and deficient mismatch repair (dMMR) status are interchangeable biomarkers indicating loss of DNA mismatch repair function. MSI-H/dMMR is caused by germline mutations in MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2 (Lynch syndrome) or by somatic MLH1 promoter hypermethylation. Approximately 15% of colorectal and 20-30% of endometrial cancers are MSI-H. Pembrolizumab was the first drug to receive tissue-agnostic FDA approval for MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors. MSI-H tumors have high neoantigen burden, dense lymphocytic infiltration, and respond well to anti-PD-1 therapy. Testing is performed by PCR (Bethesda panel), NGS, or IHC for MMR protein expression. Universal MSI/MMR testing is recommended for all colorectal cancers per NCCN guidelines.
predictive_value: Response to immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, dostarlimab)
testing_method: PCR (Bethesda markers), NGS, IHC (MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2)
clinical_cutoff: >=2/5 Bethesda markers unstable or loss of MMR protein expression by IHC

text_summary: PD-L1 Tumor Proportion Score (TPS) measures the percentage of viable tumor cells showing partial or complete membrane staining for PD-L1 at any intensity. PD-L1 TPS is a critical predictive biomarker for pembrolizumab monotherapy in NSCLC, with TPS >=50% qualifying for first-line single-agent pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-024 trial: PFS 10.3 months vs 6.0 months for chemotherapy). TPS >=1% supports pembrolizumab use in combination with chemotherapy or as second-line monotherapy. PD-L1 is assessed by IHC using FDA-approved companion diagnostics (22C3 pharmDx for pembrolizumab, 28-8 for nivolumab, SP142 for atezolizumab, SP263 for durvalumab). PD-L1 expression is dynamic and can change with treatment. PD-L1 TPS has limitations as approximately 10-20% of PD-L1-negative NSCLC patients still respond to immunotherapy.
cancer_types: NSCLC, head and neck, gastric, cervical, urothelial
predictive_value: Response to anti-PD-1/PD-L1 immunotherapy
testing_method: IHC (22C3, 28-8, SP142, SP263 antibody clones)
clinical_cutoff: >=50% for pembrolizumab monotherapy; >=1% for combination

text_summary: Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD) score is a composite biomarker measuring three independent markers of genomic scarring: loss of heterozygosity (LOH), telomeric allelic imbalance (TAI), and large-scale state transitions (LST). A high HRD score indicates impaired homologous recombination DNA repair, regardless of BRCA mutation status. The Myriad myChoice CDx HRD assay (cutoff >=42) is an FDA-approved companion diagnostic for identifying ovarian cancer patients eligible for PARP inhibitor therapy (olaparib, niraparib). HRD-positive tumors without BRCA mutations (approximately 20-30% of ovarian cancers) still benefit from PARP inhibitors, though the magnitude of benefit is generally less than in BRCA-mutant tumors. HRD testing is also being investigated in breast, prostate, and pancreatic cancers.
predictive_value: Response to PARP inhibitors (olaparib, niraparib)
testing_method: Myriad myChoice CDx (LOH + TAI + LST composite)
clinical_cutoff: >=42 (myChoice CDx scale)

text_summary: BRCA1/2 mutation status identifies germline or somatic pathogenic variants in the BRCA1 and BRCA2 homologous recombination repair genes. BRCA-positive status is the strongest single predictive biomarker for PARP inhibitor efficacy across multiple tumor types. FDA-approved PARP inhibitor indications linked to BRCA status include ovarian cancer (olaparib, rucaparib, niraparib), breast cancer (olaparib, talazoparib), prostate cancer (olaparib, rucaparib), and pancreatic cancer (olaparib maintenance). Both germline and somatic BRCA testing are recommended, as approximately 30-50% of BRCA mutations in ovarian cancer are somatic. BRCA mutations also predict sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy. NCCN guidelines recommend BRCA testing for all ovarian, high-risk breast, metastatic prostate, and pancreatic cancer patients.
predictive_value: Response to PARP inhibitors and platinum-based chemotherapy
testing_method: NGS (germline and somatic), large rearrangement analysis
clinical_cutoff: Pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants

text_summary: ALK rearrangement testing by FISH (fluorescence in situ hybridization) or NGS identifies ALK gene fusions that drive approximately 3-5% of NSCLCs. The ALK FISH break-apart probe detects rearrangements when the 3' and 5' signals are separated in >=15% of scored tumor cells. While FISH was the original FDA-approved companion diagnostic for crizotinib, NGS has become the preferred testing method as it identifies both the ALK fusion and the specific fusion partner (EML4 being most common). IHC with the Ventana ALK D5F3 antibody is an acceptable screening method with high sensitivity and specificity. Positive ALK status qualifies patients for ALK TKIs (alectinib, lorlatinib, crizotinib, ceritinib, brigatinib). ALK fusions are typically mutually exclusive with other driver mutations (EGFR, ROS1, KRAS).
predictive_value: Response to ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitors
testing_method: FISH (break-apart probe), NGS (DNA/RNA), IHC (D5F3 clone)
clinical_cutoff: >=15% cells with rearrangement by FISH; positive by validated IHC

text_summary: ROS1 rearrangement testing identifies ROS1 gene fusions present in approximately 1-2% of NSCLC. FISH using the ROS1 break-apart probe is one of the gold-standard detection methods, with positivity defined as separation of signals in >=15% of scored cells. NGS (particularly RNA-based sequencing) provides the advantage of identifying the specific fusion partner. RT-PCR-based assays can detect known fusion variants but may miss novel partners. Positive ROS1 status qualifies patients for ROS1 inhibitors including crizotinib, entrectinib, and repotrectinib. ROS1-positive NSCLC tends to occur in younger, never-smoking patients and is mutually exclusive with EGFR, ALK, and KRAS mutations. NCCN guidelines mandate ROS1 testing as part of comprehensive biomarker profiling for all advanced non-squamous NSCLC patients.
predictive_value: Response to ROS1 tyrosine kinase inhibitors
testing_method: FISH (break-apart probe), NGS (RNA-based preferred), IHC (D4D6 clone for screening)
clinical_cutoff: >=15% cells with rearrangement by FISH

text_summary: EGFR mutation testing detects activating mutations in the EGFR tyrosine kinase domain that predict sensitivity to EGFR TKIs in NSCLC. Common sensitizing mutations include exon 19 deletions (~45% of EGFR mutations) and L858R (~40%). Uncommon mutations include G719X, S768I, L861Q, and exon 20 insertions. Testing is performed by NGS (comprehensive), real-time PCR (cobas EGFR Mutation Test v2 is FDA-approved companion diagnostic), or allele-specific assays. Liquid biopsy (ctDNA) can detect EGFR mutations including the T790M resistance mutation with sensitivity of ~70-80%. EGFR mutation testing is mandatory for all advanced non-squamous NSCLC patients before initiating first-line therapy per NCCN guidelines. Positive EGFR mutation status qualifies patients for first-line osimertinib (preferred), erlotinib, gefitinib, afatinib, or dacomitinib.
predictive_value: Response to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors
testing_method: NGS, real-time PCR (cobas v2), ctDNA (liquid biopsy)
clinical_cutoff: Presence of sensitizing mutation (L858R, exon 19 del, others)

text_summary: KRAS mutation status is both a predictive biomarker (negative predictor for anti-EGFR antibodies in CRC, positive predictor for KRAS G12C inhibitors in NSCLC) and a prognostic biomarker. In colorectal cancer, extended RAS (KRAS/NRAS exons 2-4) wild-type status is required for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab). In NSCLC, KRAS G12C is specifically targetable by sotorasib and adagrasib. KRAS mutations occur in approximately 25-30% of NSCLC, 40-50% of CRC, and 90% of pancreatic cancers. Testing is performed by NGS or allele-specific PCR assays. The therascreen KRAS RGQ PCR kit is the companion diagnostic for sotorasib. KRAS mutations are mutually exclusive with other RAS/RAF mutations and with EGFR mutations in NSCLC. KRAS G12D and G12V are the next most common alleles under investigation for targeted therapy.
predictive_value: KRAS G12C: response to sotorasib/adagrasib; KRAS-mutant: resistance to anti-EGFR in CRC
testing_method: NGS, allele-specific PCR (therascreen KRAS RGQ PCR Kit)
clinical_cutoff: Presence of pathogenic KRAS mutation

text_summary: HER2 IHC and FISH testing is the standard algorithm for assessing HER2 status in breast and gastric cancers. HER2 IHC scoring follows a semi-quantitative scale: 0 (negative), 1+ (negative), 2+ (equivocal, requires reflex FISH), and 3+ (positive). HER2 FISH positivity is defined as HER2/CEP17 ratio >=2.0 with average HER2 copy number >=4.0, or ratio <2.0 with average HER2 copy number >=6.0 per cell. HER2 3+ by IHC or FISH-amplified status qualifies patients for HER2-directed therapy (trastuzumab, pertuzumab, T-DM1, T-DXd). Notably, trastuzumab deruxtecan has demonstrated efficacy in HER2-low breast cancer (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/FISH-negative) in the DESTINY-Breast04 trial, expanding the actionable definition of HER2. NCCN guidelines require HER2 testing on all newly diagnosed invasive breast cancers.
predictive_value: Response to HER2-directed therapy (trastuzumab, pertuzumab, T-DXd, T-DM1)
testing_method: IHC (HercepTest, 4B5 clone), FISH (PathVysion HER2 DNA Probe Kit)
clinical_cutoff: IHC 3+ or FISH-amplified (ratio >=2.0); HER2-low: IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/FISH-negative

text_summary: Estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR) status assessed by immunohistochemistry is a fundamental prognostic and predictive biomarker in breast cancer. ER-positive breast cancer (approximately 70-80% of breast cancers) is defined by >=1% tumor cell nuclear staining and is eligible for endocrine therapy (tamoxifen, aromatase inhibitors, CDK4/6 inhibitors in combination). ER/PR testing is mandatory on all newly diagnosed invasive breast cancers and recurrences per ASCO/CAP guidelines. PR expression is prognostic within HR-positive disease and may also predict endocrine therapy benefit. Loss of ER/PR expression at recurrence suggests clonal evolution or treatment-related changes and warrants retesting. ER/PR-negative, HER2-negative breast cancer (triple-negative) has the most limited targeted therapy options and worst prognosis among breast cancer subtypes.
predictive_value: Response to endocrine therapy (tamoxifen, aromatase inhibitors, fulvestrant, CDK4/6 inhibitors)
testing_method: IHC (SP1, 1D5, 6F11 clones for ER; 1A6, 1E2 clones for PR)
clinical_cutoff: >=1% tumor cells with nuclear staining (ASCO/CAP guideline)

text_summary: Ki-67 is a nuclear protein expressed in proliferating cells and measured by IHC as the percentage of positively staining tumor cells (Ki-67 labeling index). In breast cancer, Ki-67 is used to distinguish luminal A (low Ki-67, <20%) from luminal B (high Ki-67, >=20%) molecular subtypes, influencing adjuvant treatment decisions. Higher Ki-67 is associated with worse prognosis but may predict greater benefit from chemotherapy. The monarchE trial validated Ki-67 >=20% as an eligibility criterion for adjuvant abemaciclib in HR-positive/HER2-negative early breast cancer. Ki-67 scoring reproducibility remains challenging, and standardization efforts (International Ki-67 in Breast Cancer Working Group) are ongoing. Ki-67 is also used in neuroendocrine tumors for grading (G1 <3%, G2 3-20%, G3 >20%). Pre-analytical and analytical variables can significantly affect Ki-67 results.
predictive_value: Prognosis in breast cancer; grading in neuroendocrine tumors; adjuvant abemaciclib eligibility
testing_method: IHC (MIB-1 antibody clone)
clinical_cutoff: >=20% for luminal B classification in breast cancer; >=20% for abemaciclib eligibility

text_summary: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is a glycoprotein tumor marker measured in serum by immunoassay. CEA is the most widely used serum biomarker in colorectal cancer for post-surgical surveillance, monitoring treatment response, and detecting recurrence. Normal CEA levels are less than 5 ng/mL in non-smokers and less than 10 ng/mL in smokers. Elevated CEA at diagnosis correlates with tumor burden and is an independent prognostic factor in colorectal cancer. ASCO and NCCN guidelines recommend serial CEA monitoring every 3-6 months for 5 years after curative resection of stage II-III CRC. Rising CEA levels may indicate recurrence 3-6 months before radiographic detection. CEA has limited diagnostic sensitivity and specificity and should not be used for cancer screening. CEA is also elevated in pancreatic, gastric, breast, and lung cancers but is less validated in these settings.
predictive_value: Surveillance marker for colorectal cancer recurrence; treatment response monitoring
testing_method: Serum immunoassay (ECLIA, CLIA)
clinical_cutoff: <5 ng/mL (normal); rising trend indicates recurrence

text_summary: CA-125 (Cancer Antigen 125, MUC16) is a serum glycoprotein biomarker primarily used in ovarian cancer for diagnosis, monitoring treatment response, and surveillance. Elevated CA-125 (>35 U/mL) is found in approximately 80% of advanced epithelial ovarian cancers and 50% of early-stage disease. Serial CA-125 measurements during chemotherapy assess treatment response, with a greater than 50% decline predicting favorable outcomes. The GCIG criteria define CA-125 response as >=50% decrease from pre-treatment level maintained for >=28 days. CA-125 is also used for recurrence surveillance, where rising levels often precede clinical or radiographic evidence of progression by 3-6 months. Limitations include false elevations in endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, liver disease, and other benign conditions. NCCN guidelines recommend CA-125 monitoring before each cycle of chemotherapy and during surveillance.
predictive_value: Diagnosis, treatment response, and recurrence monitoring in ovarian cancer
testing_method: Serum immunoassay (ECLIA, CLIA)
clinical_cutoff: >35 U/mL (upper limit of normal)

text_summary: Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a serine protease produced by prostatic epithelial cells and measured in serum as the primary biomarker for prostate cancer screening, diagnosis, staging, and treatment monitoring. PSA levels above 4.0 ng/mL traditionally trigger further evaluation, though age-adjusted and risk-stratified cutoffs are increasingly used. PSA kinetics (PSA velocity, PSA doubling time) provide additional prognostic information in post-treatment surveillance. PSA nadir after radiation therapy predicts recurrence risk (Phoenix criteria: nadir + 2 ng/mL defines biochemical failure). After radical prostatectomy, any detectable PSA (>0.1 ng/mL) suggests residual or recurrent disease. PSA screening remains controversial due to overdiagnosis concerns, with USPSTF recommending shared decision-making for men aged 55-69. PSA also has utility in monitoring castration-resistant prostate cancer response to novel hormonal agents and chemotherapy.
predictive_value: Screening, diagnosis, staging, and treatment response monitoring in prostate cancer
testing_method: Serum immunoassay (ECLIA, CLIA)
clinical_cutoff: >4.0 ng/mL (traditional); age-adjusted thresholds vary

text_summary: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is a serum glycoprotein biomarker used in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and germ cell tumors. In HCC, AFP >400 ng/mL in the setting of a liver mass is highly suggestive of HCC and may obviate the need for biopsy per AASLD guidelines. AFP is used for HCC surveillance in high-risk populations (cirrhosis, chronic hepatitis B) every 6 months in combination with ultrasound. AFP levels correlate with tumor size, vascular invasion, and prognosis in HCC. In germ cell tumors, AFP is produced by yolk sac tumor elements and is used in combination with beta-hCG and LDH for diagnosis, staging (IGCCC classification), treatment monitoring, and recurrence surveillance. AFP half-life is approximately 5-7 days, and persistent elevation after chemotherapy suggests residual viable tumor. AFP-L3 fraction is a more specific HCC biomarker under investigation.
predictive_value: Diagnosis, staging, and surveillance in HCC and germ cell tumors
testing_method: Serum immunoassay (ECLIA, CLIA)
clinical_cutoff: >400 ng/mL (HCC diagnosis); normal <10 ng/mL

text_summary: Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) is cell-free DNA shed by tumors into the bloodstream, enabling non-invasive liquid biopsy for genotyping, treatment monitoring, and minimal residual disease (MRD) detection. ctDNA-based assays (Guardant360, FoundationOne Liquid CDx) are FDA-approved companion diagnostics for identifying actionable mutations when tissue biopsy is unavailable or insufficient. Serial ctDNA monitoring can detect resistance mutations (e.g., EGFR T790M, ESR1 mutations) months before clinical progression. Post-surgical ctDNA detection (MRD-positive) is a powerful prognostic biomarker predicting recurrence across multiple cancer types (CRC, NSCLC, breast). The CIRCULATE and DYNAMIC trials are evaluating ctDNA-guided adjuvant therapy decisions in CRC. ctDNA clearance during treatment correlates with favorable outcomes. Technical approaches include PCR-based (ddPCR), targeted NGS, and whole-genome sequencing of cell-free DNA.
predictive_value: Genotyping, resistance monitoring, MRD detection, treatment response assessment
testing_method: Targeted NGS (Guardant360, FoundationOne Liquid CDx), ddPCR, WGS
clinical_cutoff: Variant allele frequency (VAF) detection limit ~0.1-0.5% for targeted panels

text_summary: Oncotype DX is a 21-gene RT-qPCR-based prognostic assay that generates a Recurrence Score (RS) ranging from 0 to 100 for HR-positive/HER2-negative early-stage breast cancer. The TAILORx trial validated RS thresholds: RS 0-10 (low risk, endocrine therapy alone), RS 11-25 (intermediate risk, endocrine therapy alone for most patients aged >50), RS 26-100 (high risk, chemotherapy + endocrine therapy). The RxPONDER trial demonstrated that postmenopausal women with 1-3 positive nodes and RS <=25 do not benefit from chemotherapy. Oncotype DX is recommended by NCCN and ASCO guidelines for treatment planning in node-negative and limited node-positive (1-3 nodes), HR-positive/HER2-negative, early breast cancer. The assay is performed on FFPE tumor tissue and helps avoid unnecessary chemotherapy in approximately 50% of eligible patients.
predictive_value: Predicts chemotherapy benefit in early-stage HR+/HER2- breast cancer
testing_method: 21-gene RT-qPCR assay on FFPE tissue
clinical_cutoff: RS 0-25: endocrine only (most patients); RS 26-100: chemo + endocrine

text_summary: MammaPrint is a 70-gene microarray-based prognostic assay that classifies early-stage breast cancer as genomic low-risk or high-risk, guiding the decision to add chemotherapy to endocrine therapy. The landmark MINDACT trial (6,693 patients) demonstrated that clinical high-risk / genomic low-risk patients had excellent 5-year distant metastasis-free survival of 94.7% without chemotherapy, establishing that approximately 46% of clinical high-risk patients can safely avoid chemotherapy. MammaPrint is applicable to patients with stage I-II, ER-positive or ER-negative, HER2-negative breast cancer, 1-3 positive nodes, and tumor size <=5cm. Unlike Oncotype DX, MammaPrint uses fresh or FFPE tissue and provides a binary (low/high risk) rather than continuous score. MammaPrint is endorsed by ASCO and St. Gallen guidelines for adjuvant treatment decision-making.
predictive_value: Identifies patients who can safely omit adjuvant chemotherapy
testing_method: 70-gene microarray (Agendia)
clinical_cutoff: Binary: genomic low-risk vs high-risk

text_summary: Circulating tumor cells (CTCs) are intact tumor cells shed from primary or metastatic tumors into the bloodstream. The CellSearch system is the only FDA-cleared CTC enumeration platform and is validated in metastatic breast, prostate, and colorectal cancers. In metastatic breast cancer, >=5 CTCs per 7.5 mL blood is associated with significantly worse progression-free and overall survival. CTC count at baseline and changes during treatment provide real-time prognostic information. CTC enumeration can guide treatment intensity decisions in metastatic settings. Beyond enumeration, molecular characterization of CTCs (gene expression, protein markers, mutation profiling) can provide insights into tumor heterogeneity and drug resistance mechanisms. Emerging CTC technologies include microfluidic devices, size-based filtration, and single-cell sequencing. CTCs are being investigated as MRD biomarkers in early-stage cancers.
predictive_value: Prognosis and treatment response monitoring in metastatic cancers
testing_method: CellSearch system (EpCAM-based immunomagnetic capture)
clinical_cutoff: >=5 CTCs per 7.5 mL blood (unfavorable prognosis in breast); >=5 in prostate

text_summary: Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) are immune cells present within the tumor microenvironment, assessed by standard H&E histology as the percentage of stromal area occupied by lymphocytic infiltrate. High stromal TILs (>=50-60%) in triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) are a strong favorable prognostic biomarker, associated with improved disease-free and overall survival. Each 10% increase in TILs is associated with a 15-17% reduction in recurrence risk in TNBC. TILs also predict response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy and immunotherapy in breast cancer. The International TILs Working Group has published standardized scoring guidelines. TIL assessment is being incorporated into clinical trials as a stratification factor and potential predictive biomarker for immunotherapy in breast and other cancers. TILs complement PD-L1 and TMB as immune biomarkers for checkpoint inhibitor therapy selection.
predictive_value: Favorable prognosis in TNBC; potential predictor of immunotherapy response
testing_method: H&E histology (stromal TIL scoring per International TILs WG guidelines)
clinical_cutoff: >=50-60% stromal TILs (strong favorable prognosis in TNBC)

text_summary: Folate receptor alpha (FRalpha) is a glycosylphosphatidylinositol-anchored membrane protein that mediates cellular folate uptake and is overexpressed in approximately 80% of high-grade serous ovarian cancers and at lower frequencies in endometrial, TNBC, and NSCLC. FRalpha expression is a predictive biomarker for mirvetuximab soravtansine, an anti-FRalpha ADC approved for platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. The VENTANA FOLR1 (26B3) RxDx assay is the FDA-approved companion diagnostic, using a Proportion Score (PS) system where PS2+ (moderate-to-strong membrane staining in >=75% of tumor cells) defines eligibility. The MIRASOL trial demonstrated that FRalpha-high patients derive the greatest benefit from mirvetuximab soravtansine (PFS 5.6 vs 4.0 months, OS 16.5 vs 12.8 months). FRalpha expression correlates with serous histology and high-grade disease. Normal tissue expression is restricted to the apical surface of polarized epithelial cells, limiting off-target toxicity.
predictive_value: Response to mirvetuximab soravtansine in FRalpha-positive platinum-resistant ovarian cancer
testing_method: IHC (VENTANA FOLR1 26B3 RxDx assay)
clinical_cutoff: PS2+: moderate-to-strong staining in >=75% of viable tumor cells

text_summary: Nectin-4 is a type I transmembrane protein of the immunoglobulin superfamily that mediates cell adhesion and is broadly expressed in approximately 60-70% of urothelial carcinomas at high levels, with additional expression in breast, lung, pancreatic, and gastric cancers. Nectin-4 serves as the therapeutic target for enfortumab vedotin, an anti-Nectin-4 ADC with MMAE payload. Unlike many ADC biomarkers, Nectin-4 expression testing by IHC is not required for enfortumab vedotin eligibility because of the near-universal expression in urothelial carcinoma. The EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39 trial established enfortumab vedotin plus pembrolizumab as first-line therapy in urothelial carcinoma regardless of Nectin-4 expression level. Nectin-4 loss through downregulation is an emerging resistance mechanism to enfortumab vedotin. H-score IHC assessment can quantify expression, with most urothelial carcinomas showing H-scores greater than 150. Research is ongoing to evaluate Nectin-4 as a patient selection biomarker in non-urothelial cancers.
predictive_value: Therapeutic target for enfortumab vedotin (no companion diagnostic required in urothelial carcinoma)
testing_method: IHC (research setting; not required for clinical use in urothelial carcinoma)
clinical_cutoff: No established clinical cutoff for urothelial carcinoma (near-universal expression)

text_summary: Claudin 18.2 (CLDN18.2) is a tight junction protein normally expressed in differentiated gastric mucosal cells that becomes aberrantly accessible on the tumor cell surface in approximately 38-40% of gastric/GEJ adenocarcinomas and 50-60% of pancreatic cancers. Loss of cell polarity during malignant transformation exposes CLDN18.2 epitopes, making it targetable by zolbetuximab. The VENTANA CLDN18 (43-14A) RxDx assay is the FDA-approved companion diagnostic. Positivity is defined as moderate-to-strong membranous staining in >=75% of tumor cells. The SPOTLIGHT and GLOW trials demonstrated that CLDN18.2-positive patients benefit from adding zolbetuximab to first-line chemotherapy in gastric/GEJ cancer. CLDN18.2 testing should be performed alongside HER2 testing, as zolbetuximab is indicated only for HER2-negative disease. CLDN18.2 expression is mutually informative with but independent of MSI, PD-L1, and HER2 status. Testing on biopsy specimens is adequate, though intratumoral heterogeneity may affect results.
predictive_value: Response to zolbetuximab in CLDN18.2-positive gastric/GEJ adenocarcinoma
testing_method: IHC (VENTANA CLDN18 43-14A RxDx assay)
clinical_cutoff: Moderate-to-strong membranous staining in >=75% of tumor cells

text_summary: Measurable residual disease (MRD) in hematologic malignancies refers to the detection of residual cancer cells below the threshold of conventional morphologic assessment (typically <5% blasts). MRD assessment is a powerful prognostic and predictive biomarker in acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), acute myeloid leukemia (AML), chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), and multiple myeloma. In CLL, MRD negativity (undetectable MRD at 10^-4 sensitivity) after venetoclax-based therapy strongly predicts prolonged PFS and is being used as a treatment endpoint in clinical trials. In AML, MRD by multiparameter flow cytometry (MFC) or molecular methods (NPM1 qPCR, NGS) guides post-remission therapy decisions. In ALL, MRD-positive status after induction guides intensification or blinatumomab consolidation. The two primary detection methods are multiparameter flow cytometry (sensitivity 10^-4 to 10^-5) and molecular approaches including NGS-based clonoSEQ (sensitivity 10^-6). NCCN guidelines recommend MRD assessment at key treatment milestones.
predictive_value: Predicts relapse risk and guides post-remission therapy; treatment endpoint in CLL trials
testing_method: Multiparameter flow cytometry (MFC), NGS (clonoSEQ), qPCR (NPM1, BCR-ABL1)
clinical_cutoff: Undetectable MRD at 10^-4 (flow) or 10^-6 (NGS) sensitivity

text_summary: Tumor mutational burden (TMB) can be assessed from tissue (tTMB) or blood (bTMB) samples, with important concordance considerations. Tissue TMB, measured by comprehensive genomic profiling panels (FoundationOne CDx, MSK-IMPACT), is the FDA-approved approach for pembrolizumab eligibility (>=10 mut/Mb). Blood-based TMB (bTMB) is calculated from ctDNA using panels such as Guardant360 CDx and offers advantages of non-invasive serial monitoring and avoidance of tissue biopsy challenges. However, bTMB and tTMB show moderate correlation (r ~0.6-0.7), and the optimal bTMB threshold for immunotherapy prediction differs from tissue-based cutoffs. The BFAST trial explored bTMB >=16 as a threshold for atezolizumab benefit in NSCLC. Key limitations of bTMB include reduced sensitivity in low tumor shedding contexts and potential confounding by clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP). Current NCCN guidelines recommend tissue-based TMB when available, with bTMB as an alternative when tissue is insufficient.
predictive_value: Response to immune checkpoint inhibitors; tissue TMB FDA-approved, blood TMB under investigation
testing_method: Tissue: CGP panels (FoundationOne CDx, MSK-IMPACT); Blood: ctDNA panels (Guardant360 CDx)
clinical_cutoff: Tissue: >=10 mut/Mb (FDA-approved); Blood: >=16 mut/Mb (investigational BFAST threshold)

text_summary: Genomic instability score (GIS) is a composite biomarker that quantifies genomic scarring from homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) by measuring three independent metrics: loss of heterozygosity (LOH), telomeric allelic imbalance (TAI), and large-scale state transitions (LST). The Myriad myChoice CDx HRD assay calculates a GIS combining these three components with a clinical threshold of >=42 to define HRD-positive status. GIS is an FDA-approved companion diagnostic for PARP inhibitor eligibility in ovarian cancer, identifying both BRCA-mutant and BRCA wild-type HRD-positive patients. In the PRIMA/ENGOT-OV26 trial, niraparib maintenance significantly improved PFS in the HRD-positive population (21.9 vs 10.4 months). The GIS captures cumulative genomic damage from HRD regardless of the specific causative gene defect, including cases with BRCA1 promoter methylation, RAD51C loss, or other HRR gene alterations. GIS testing is performed on FFPE tumor tissue and requires adequate tumor cellularity.
predictive_value: Response to PARP inhibitors in HRD-positive tumors regardless of BRCA mutation status
testing_method: Myriad myChoice CDx (LOH + TAI + LST composite score)
clinical_cutoff: >=42 on myChoice CDx scale defines HRD-positive

text_summary: DLL3 (Delta-like ligand 3) is an atypical inhibitory Notch pathway ligand aberrantly expressed on the cell surface of approximately 80-85% of small cell lung cancers (SCLC), 50-70% of large cell neuroendocrine carcinomas, and other high-grade neuroendocrine tumors, while being virtually absent from normal adult tissues. DLL3 surface expression in SCLC results from neuroendocrine lineage-specific transcriptional programs driven by ASCL1. DLL3 is the therapeutic target for tarlatamab (DLL3xCD3 BiTE antibody) and was previously targeted by rovalpituzumab tesirine (Rova-T), which was discontinued due to insufficient efficacy. DLL3 IHC testing is available using antibodies such as the SP347 clone. While DLL3 testing is not currently required for tarlatamab eligibility in SCLC due to high prevalence, it may become relevant for patient selection in non-SCLC neuroendocrine tumors. DLL3 expression is retained in the majority of SCLC tumors even after multiple lines of therapy.
cancer_types: SCLC, large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma, neuroendocrine tumors
predictive_value: Therapeutic target for tarlatamab (DLL3xCD3 BiTE); high prevalence in SCLC
testing_method: IHC (SP347 clone), RNA expression analysis
clinical_cutoff: No established clinical cutoff for SCLC (near-universal expression); >=1% for research studies

text_summary: TROP2 (Trophoblast cell-surface antigen 2, encoded by TACSTD2) is a transmembrane glycoprotein broadly expressed across epithelial malignancies, with high expression found in approximately 80-90% of TNBC, 60-80% of HR-positive breast cancers, 60-70% of urothelial carcinomas, and significant expression in NSCLC, gastric, and colorectal cancers. TROP2 promotes tumor growth through intracellular domain signaling, cyclin D1 upregulation, and ERK activation. TROP2 is the target for sacituzumab govitecan (anti-TROP2 ADC) and datopotamab deruxtecan (in development). Notably, sacituzumab govitecan is approved without a companion diagnostic for TROP2 expression, as clinical activity has been observed across the range of TROP2 expression levels, likely due to the high drug-to-antibody ratio and bystander killing effect. IHC scoring with H-score may help identify patients most likely to benefit, but prospective validation is needed. TROP2 expression levels may evolve under therapeutic pressure.
cancer_types: breast (TNBC, HR+), urothelial, NSCLC, gastric, colorectal
predictive_value: Therapeutic target for sacituzumab govitecan; no companion diagnostic currently required
testing_method: IHC (SP419 clone, research setting); not required for clinical use
clinical_cutoff: No established clinical cutoff (broad expression; no companion diagnostic required)

text_summary: EZH2 mutation status identifies gain-of-function mutations in the EZH2 histone methyltransferase that are found in approximately 7-12% of follicular lymphomas (FL), 22% of germinal center B-cell diffuse large B-cell lymphomas (GCB-DLBCL), and rarely in other cancers. Hotspot EZH2 mutations (Y646N/F/H/S/C, A687V, A692V) in the SET catalytic domain result in neomorphic activity, increasing H3K27me3 levels and silencing tumor suppressor genes. EZH2 mutation status is a predictive biomarker for tazemetostat, an EZH2 inhibitor approved for relapsed/refractory FL. The EZH2-mutant FL cohort showed ORR of 69% versus 34% for EZH2 wild-type, though tazemetostat is approved for both populations. EZH2 mutation testing is performed by NGS, with specific interrogation of the SET domain hotspots. NCCN guidelines recommend EZH2 testing in FL patients being considered for tazemetostat therapy. EZH2 overexpression without mutation is common in solid tumors and may also predict tazemetostat sensitivity in specific contexts.
predictive_value: Response to tazemetostat (EZH2 inhibitor); higher response rates in EZH2-mutant FL
testing_method: NGS (targeted sequencing of EZH2 SET domain hotspots)
clinical_cutoff: Presence of activating EZH2 SET domain mutation (Y646, A687V, A692V)

text_summary: NPM1 (Nucleophosmin 1) mutation status is the most common genetic alteration in acute myeloid leukemia, occurring in approximately 30% of adult AML cases and approximately 50-60% of cytogenetically normal AML. NPM1 mutations involve 4-base pair insertions in exon 12 (most commonly Type A: TCTG duplication), resulting in aberrant cytoplasmic localization of the nucleophosmin protein, which can be detected by IHC as cytoplasmic positivity. NPM1-mutant AML without FLT3-ITD (or with low FLT3-ITD allelic ratio) is classified as favorable risk per the 2022 ELN risk stratification. NPM1-mutant AML responds well to intensive chemotherapy with high complete remission rates of 80-90%, and these patients may not benefit from allogeneic stem cell transplantation in first remission. NPM1 mutation is a stable molecular marker during disease course and is the preferred MRD monitoring target by RT-qPCR, with NPM1 MRD positivity after consolidation strongly predicting relapse. Loss of NPM1 mutation at relapse occurs in approximately 10% of cases and may signal clonal evolution.
predictive_value: Favorable prognosis in AML without FLT3-ITD; preferred MRD monitoring target; guides transplant decisions
testing_method: NGS, fragment analysis (capillary electrophoresis), RT-qPCR (MRD), IHC (cytoplasmic NPM1)
clinical_cutoff: Presence of NPM1 exon 12 insertion mutation; MRD: <0.1% NPM1 mutant transcripts by RT-qPCR

text_summary: FLT3-ITD (FMS-like tyrosine kinase 3 internal tandem duplication) allelic ratio is a critical prognostic and predictive biomarker in acute myeloid leukemia, present in approximately 25-30% of AML cases. The FLT3-ITD allelic ratio (mutant-to-wild-type signal ratio) stratifies patients into low (<0.5) and high (>=0.5) allelic ratio groups, with high allelic ratio conferring adverse prognosis independent of co-occurring mutations. The 2022 ELN risk classification incorporates FLT3-ITD status with NPM1 co-mutation: NPM1-mutant with FLT3-ITD (any allelic ratio) is now classified as favorable, while NPM1-wild-type with FLT3-ITD is classified as intermediate risk. Midostaurin added to standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy significantly improves overall survival in FLT3-mutant AML (RATIFY trial: OS 74.7 vs 25.6 months). Gilteritinib is FDA-approved for relapsed/refractory FLT3-mutant AML. Quizartinib, a selective FLT3 inhibitor, is approved for FLT3-ITD-positive newly diagnosed AML. FLT3-ITD testing by fragment analysis or NGS should be performed at diagnosis and relapse.
predictive_value: Predicts response to FLT3 inhibitors (midostaurin, gilteritinib, quizartinib); adverse prognosis at high allelic ratio
testing_method: Fragment analysis (capillary electrophoresis), NGS, allele-specific PCR
clinical_cutoff: Allelic ratio <0.5 (low) vs >=0.5 (high); any FLT3-ITD qualifies for FLT3 inhibitor therapy

text_summary: BCR-ABL1 transcript level quantified by real-time quantitative PCR on the International Scale (IS) is the cornerstone biomarker for monitoring treatment response in chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). The BCR-ABL1 fusion oncogene, resulting from t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome translocation, produces a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives CML pathogenesis. Quantitative RT-PCR monitoring on the IS enables standardized response milestones: early molecular response (EMR, BCR-ABL1 <=10% IS at 3 months), major molecular response (MMR, BCR-ABL1 <=0.1% IS), deep molecular response MR4 (<=0.01% IS), and MR4.5 (<=0.0032% IS). Failure to achieve EMR at 3 months predicts inferior long-term outcomes and warrants treatment change. Sustained deep molecular response (MR4 or deeper) for at least 2 years is a prerequisite for treatment-free remission (TFR) attempts. BCR-ABL1 kinase domain mutation analysis by Sanger sequencing or NGS identifies resistance mutations guiding TKI selection, with T315I being the most clinically significant gatekeeper mutation requiring ponatinib or asciminib.
predictive_value: Treatment response monitoring; milestone-based assessment at 3, 6, 12 months; TFR eligibility
testing_method: Quantitative RT-PCR (International Scale), ABL1 kinase domain mutation analysis (Sanger/NGS)
clinical_cutoff: EMR: <=10% IS at 3 months; MMR: <=0.1% IS; MR4: <=0.01% IS; MR4.5: <=0.0032% IS

text_summary: JAK2 V617F allele burden is a diagnostic, prognostic, and monitoring biomarker in myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPNs). The JAK2 V617F somatic point mutation (substitution of valine to phenylalanine at position 617 in the pseudokinase domain) is found in approximately 95% of polycythemia vera (PV), 55-65% of essential thrombocythemia (ET), and 55-65% of primary myelofibrosis (PMF). The variant allele frequency (VAF) of JAK2 V617F correlates with disease phenotype: higher allele burden (>50%) is associated with PV phenotype, splenomegaly, thrombotic risk, and risk of transformation to myelofibrosis. In ET, lower JAK2 V617F allele burden is typical. Serial monitoring of JAK2 V617F VAF can assess molecular response to JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib, fedratinib) and interferon-alpha therapy. A sustained reduction in JAK2 V617F allele burden during treatment correlates with improved outcomes. Testing is performed by allele-specific quantitative PCR or digital droplet PCR with sensitivity to detect VAF as low as 0.1%. JAK2 V617F-negative MPN patients should be tested for CALR and MPL mutations.
cancer_types: polycythemia vera, essential thrombocythemia, primary myelofibrosis
predictive_value: Diagnosis of MPN; allele burden correlates with disease phenotype and thrombotic risk; molecular response monitoring
testing_method: Allele-specific qPCR, digital droplet PCR (ddPCR), NGS
clinical_cutoff: Any detectable level diagnostic for MPN; VAF >50% associated with PV phenotype and higher thrombotic risk

text_summary: HPV (human papillomavirus) and p16 immunohistochemistry status are essential biomarkers in oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (OPSCC) and cervical cancer. In OPSCC, HPV-positive (primarily HPV-16) tumors represent approximately 70-80% of cases in developed countries and have dramatically better prognosis than HPV-negative tumors, with 3-year overall survival of 82% vs 57%. p16 IHC is used as a surrogate marker for transcriptionally active HPV infection, with diffuse strong nuclear and cytoplasmic staining in >=70% of tumor cells defining positivity. The AJCC 8th edition staging system created separate staging classifications for HPV-positive and HPV-negative oropharyngeal cancer, downstaging HPV-positive tumors to reflect their superior outcomes. In cervical cancer, high-risk HPV testing (types 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58) is used for screening, triage, and surveillance after treatment. HPV-positive OPSCC is being investigated in de-escalation trials to reduce treatment morbidity while maintaining excellent outcomes. HPV testing methods include p16 IHC, HPV DNA ISH, HPV PCR, and RNA-based assays.
cancer_types: oropharyngeal SCC, cervical, anal, vaginal, vulvar, penile
predictive_value: Favorable prognosis in HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer; staging determinant; screening biomarker in cervical cancer
testing_method: p16 IHC (E6H4 clone), HPV DNA ISH, HPV PCR (cobas HPV test), RNA-based assays
clinical_cutoff: p16 IHC: diffuse strong staining in >=70% of tumor cells; HPV DNA: detection of high-risk HPV types

text_summary: AR-V7 (androgen receptor splice variant 7) is a constitutively active truncated form of the androgen receptor that lacks the ligand-binding domain, rendering it resistant to androgen receptor signaling inhibitors (ARSIs) such as enzalutamide and abiraterone. AR-V7 is detected in circulating tumor cells (CTCs) of approximately 18-30% of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) patients, with prevalence increasing after prior ARSI exposure. The PROPHECY trial demonstrated that AR-V7-positive CTCs predict poor response to enzalutamide and abiraterone (PSA response rate <10% in AR-V7-positive vs ~40-60% in AR-V7-negative patients) but preserved sensitivity to taxane chemotherapy (docetaxel, cabazitaxel). AR-V7 detection can guide treatment selection between ARSI and taxane in the mCRPC setting. Testing is performed on CTCs using the Johns Hopkins AR-V7 nuclear protein assay or RT-PCR. AR-V7 status is dynamic and can emerge during treatment, warranting serial assessment. AR-V7 positivity also correlates with shorter overall survival independent of treatment choice.
predictive_value: Predicts resistance to enzalutamide/abiraterone; preserved sensitivity to taxane chemotherapy
testing_method: CTC-based nuclear protein assay (Johns Hopkins), RT-PCR on CTCs, immunofluorescence
clinical_cutoff: Any detectable AR-V7 nuclear protein in CTCs defines positivity

text_summary: PSMA (prostate-specific membrane antigen, also known as FOLH1/glutamate carboxypeptidase II) is a type II transmembrane glycoprotein highly expressed on prostate cancer cells, with expression increasing with disease progression, castration resistance, and metastatic spread. PSMA expression is assessed by PSMA PET/CT imaging (using 68Ga-PSMA-11 or 18F-DCFPyL radiotracers) rather than tissue-based testing. PSMA PET/CT has revolutionized prostate cancer staging with superior sensitivity and specificity compared to conventional imaging. PSMA-positive disease on PET/CT is a prerequisite for lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto) therapy, a radioligand therapy FDA-approved for mCRPC after ARSI and taxane treatment based on the VISION trial (OS 15.3 vs 11.3 months). Approximately 80-90% of mCRPC patients have PSMA-positive disease. PSMA-negative disease or heterogeneous expression with high FDG-avid/PSMA-low lesions is associated with neuroendocrine differentiation and poor prognosis. PSMA-targeted diagnostics and therapeutics (theranostics) represent a paradigm for precision nuclear medicine.
predictive_value: Eligibility for lutetium-177 PSMA-617 radioligand therapy; staging by PSMA PET/CT
testing_method: PSMA PET/CT (68Ga-PSMA-11, 18F-DCFPyL), IHC (research setting)
clinical_cutoff: PSMA-positive uptake on PET/CT (visual assessment, SUVmax); PSMA PET-positive in at least one metastatic site

text_summary: PTEN (phosphatase and tensin homolog) loss assessed by immunohistochemistry is a prognostic and emerging predictive biomarker across multiple cancer types. PTEN is a lipid phosphatase that negatively regulates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway by dephosphorylating PIP3. PTEN loss (through genomic deletion, mutation, or epigenetic silencing) occurs in approximately 40-50% of endometrial cancers, 30-40% of prostate cancers, 25-30% of glioblastomas, 15-20% of breast cancers, and 10-15% of colorectal cancers. PTEN loss by IHC is defined as complete absence of PTEN protein staining in tumor cells with retained staining in internal control stromal and endothelial cells. In prostate cancer, PTEN loss is associated with higher Gleason grade, increased risk of metastasis, and shorter time to castration resistance. PTEN loss may predict sensitivity to AKT inhibitors (capivasertib) and PI3K pathway inhibitors. In the CAPItello-291 trial, capivasertib plus fulvestrant showed enhanced benefit in the PTEN-altered subgroup of HR-positive breast cancer. PTEN loss may also predict reduced benefit from anti-EGFR therapy in colorectal cancer.
predictive_value: Adverse prognosis; potential predictor of response to AKT/PI3K pathway inhibitors
testing_method: IHC (138G6 clone), FISH (genomic deletion), NGS (mutations)
clinical_cutoff: Complete absence of PTEN protein in tumor cells with retained stromal staining by IHC

text_summary: ARID1A (AT-rich interactive domain 1A) loss by immunohistochemistry identifies tumors with inactivating mutations in the ARID1A subunit of the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex. ARID1A is one of the most frequently mutated genes across all cancers, with loss occurring in approximately 46-57% of ovarian clear cell carcinomas, 30-40% of endometrial cancers, 8-27% of gastric cancers, 10-15% of cholangiocarcinomas, and 5-10% of colorectal cancers. ARID1A loss results in unopposed EZH2-mediated transcriptional repression, creating a therapeutic vulnerability to EZH2 inhibitors. ARID1A-deficient tumors also exhibit impaired DNA damage repair, potentially sensitizing them to PARP inhibitors and ATR inhibitors. In ovarian clear cell carcinoma, ARID1A loss is an early clonal event and is associated with resistance to platinum-based chemotherapy. ARID1A loss also alters the tumor immune microenvironment, increasing T-cell infiltration and PD-L1 expression in some contexts, which may predict immunotherapy benefit. IHC using the HPA005456 or D2A8U antibody clones detects loss of nuclear ARID1A expression.
predictive_value: Potential sensitivity to EZH2 inhibitors, PARP inhibitors, and immunotherapy; platinum resistance in ovarian clear cell
testing_method: IHC (HPA005456, D2A8U clones), NGS (truncating mutations)
clinical_cutoff: Complete loss of nuclear ARID1A expression in tumor cells with retained stromal staining

text_summary: The Homologous Recombination Deficiency Genomic Instability Score (HRD-GIS) is a composite genomic scar biomarker that quantifies cumulative DNA damage from defective homologous recombination repair by integrating three genome-wide measures: loss of heterozygosity (LOH), telomeric allelic imbalance (TAI), and large-scale state transitions (LST). Unlike direct HRR gene mutation testing, HRD-GIS captures the functional consequence of HRD regardless of the underlying mechanism, including BRCA1 promoter methylation, RAD51C/D mutations, PALB2 loss, and other non-BRCA HRR defects that account for approximately 20-30% of HRD-positive ovarian cancers. The Myriad myChoice CDx reports a combined GIS score with a validated clinical cutoff of >=42 for HRD positivity. The PAOLA-1 trial demonstrated that HRD-GIS-positive patients benefit from olaparib plus bevacizumab maintenance (PFS 37.2 months for HRD-positive vs 17.7 months for HRD-negative). Emerging alternative HRD assays include the FoundationOne CDx LOH score (>=16%), the SOPHiA DDM HRD assay, and functional RAD51 focus assays that assess real-time HR competence rather than historical genomic scars.
predictive_value: Identifies HRD-positive tumors for PARP inhibitor eligibility regardless of BRCA status
testing_method: Myriad myChoice CDx (LOH+TAI+LST), FoundationOne CDx (LOH), SOPHiA DDM HRD, RAD51 functional assay
clinical_cutoff: myChoice CDx GIS >=42; FoundationOne LOH >=16%; functional RAD51 negativity

text_summary: Mutational signatures (also known as SBS or Single Base Substitution profiles) are characteristic patterns of somatic mutations imprinted in cancer genomes by specific mutagenic processes. Over 60 mutational signatures have been catalogued by the COSMIC database, each reflecting a distinct etiology. Clinically relevant signatures include SBS1 (age-related deamination), SBS2/13 (APOBEC cytidine deaminase activity, common in cervical, bladder, and breast cancers), SBS4 (tobacco smoking, associated with NSCLC), SBS6/15/20/26 (mismatch repair deficiency, correlating with MSI-H), SBS7 (UV radiation, melanoma), and SBS3 (homologous recombination deficiency, correlating with BRCA mutations). SBS3 (HRD signature) can identify tumors with functional HRD even in the absence of identifiable BRCA or HRR gene mutations, potentially expanding PARP inhibitor eligibility. APOBEC mutational signatures are associated with higher TMB and may predict immunotherapy response. Mutational signature analysis requires whole-genome or whole-exome sequencing and computational deconvolution algorithms such as SigProfiler or deconstructSigs. Clinical implementation remains largely research-based but is increasingly informing treatment decisions in molecular tumor boards.
cancer_types: pan-cancer, breast, bladder, melanoma, NSCLC, cervical
predictive_value: SBS3 identifies HRD for PARP inhibitor sensitivity; APOBEC signatures predict immunotherapy response; etiologic classification
testing_method: Whole-genome sequencing (WGS), whole-exome sequencing (WES), computational deconvolution (SigProfiler)
clinical_cutoff: Signature contribution threshold varies by algorithm; SBS3 >20% contribution suggests functional HRD

text_summary: Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) kinetics refers to the dynamic quantitative monitoring of ctDNA levels over time as a real-time biomarker of treatment response, resistance, and disease burden. Unlike single-timepoint ctDNA genotyping, ctDNA kinetics tracks changes in variant allele frequency (VAF) or ctDNA concentration across serial samples during therapy. Rapid ctDNA clearance (often defined as undetectable ctDNA or >90% VAF reduction within 2-4 weeks of treatment initiation) strongly predicts favorable PFS and OS across multiple cancer types and treatment modalities including chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy. In the DYNAMIC trial for stage II colorectal cancer, ctDNA-guided adjuvant therapy decisions reduced chemotherapy use by 50% without compromising recurrence-free survival. In metastatic NSCLC, on-treatment ctDNA dynamics at 4-8 weeks predict radiographic response and survival better than baseline PD-L1 or TMB. Molecular response (ctDNA clearance) may emerge as a surrogate endpoint for clinical trials. Key technical considerations include assay sensitivity, sampling frequency, and distinction of tumor-derived cfDNA from clonal hematopoiesis (CHIP) variants.
predictive_value: Early treatment response assessment; real-time resistance detection; ctDNA-guided adjuvant therapy decisions
testing_method: Serial ctDNA quantification by tumor-informed NGS (Signatera, RaDaR) or tumor-naive panels (Guardant360)
clinical_cutoff: ctDNA clearance: undetectable ctDNA by cycle 2-3; molecular response: >90% VAF reduction from baseline

text_summary: Minimal residual disease (MRD) detection by next-generation sequencing (NGS) in hematologic malignancies provides ultra-sensitive quantification of residual disease using patient-specific clonal immunoglobulin or T-cell receptor gene rearrangements as molecular fingerprints. The FDA-cleared clonoSEQ assay (Adaptive Biotechnologies) achieves sensitivity of 10^-6 (one malignant cell per million), approximately 100-fold more sensitive than multiparameter flow cytometry. In multiple myeloma, NGS-MRD negativity at 10^-5 or 10^-6 after treatment predicts significantly prolonged PFS and OS and is being adopted as a clinical trial endpoint. In ALL, MRD negativity by NGS after induction identifies patients with excellent outcomes who may benefit from de-escalation. In CLL, undetectable MRD by NGS after fixed-duration venetoclax-based therapy guides duration of treatment and predicts durable remission. In DLBCL, ctDNA-based MRD using tumor-specific mutations by NGS (rather than clonotype tracking) is emerging as a powerful tool for post-treatment surveillance. NGS-MRD testing requires a baseline diagnostic sample for clonotype identification and is performed on peripheral blood or bone marrow.
cancer_types: ALL, multiple myeloma, CLL, DLBCL, AML
predictive_value: Ultra-sensitive relapse prediction; treatment de-escalation guidance; clinical trial endpoint
testing_method: NGS (clonoSEQ by Adaptive Biotechnologies), targeting IgH/IgK/IgL or TCR rearrangements
clinical_cutoff: Undetectable MRD at 10^-5 or 10^-6 sensitivity; clonoSEQ lower limit of detection ~10^-6

text_summary: Tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) density quantified on H&E-stained tissue sections is a prognostic and emerging predictive biomarker across solid tumor types. TIL density assessment follows the standardized methodology published by the International Immuno-Oncology Biomarker Working Group, scoring the percentage of stromal area occupied by mononuclear inflammatory cells within the invasive tumor border. In triple-negative breast cancer, each 10% increment in stromal TILs reduces recurrence risk by 13-17%, and TIL-rich (>=50%) TNBC has 5-year OS exceeding 90% with standard chemotherapy alone. In early-stage TNBC, high TILs may identify patients who can safely avoid immunotherapy escalation. In colorectal cancer, the Immunoscore (quantifying CD3+ and CD8+ T cells at tumor center and invasive margin) provides superior prognostic stratification compared to conventional TNM staging and is endorsed by international guidelines as a complement to staging. In melanoma, brisk TIL infiltration correlates with improved response to checkpoint inhibitors. Emerging digital pathology and AI-based TIL scoring tools are improving reproducibility and enabling spatial analysis of immune cell distribution patterns.
cancer_types: breast (TNBC), colorectal, melanoma, NSCLC, ovarian, gastric
predictive_value: Favorable prognosis; potential predictor of chemotherapy and immunotherapy benefit; Immunoscore in CRC
testing_method: H&E histology (International TILs WG guidelines), Immunoscore (CD3/CD8 IHC), digital pathology/AI
clinical_cutoff: >=50% stromal TILs (strong favorable prognosis in TNBC); Immunoscore: 0-4 scale based on CD3/CD8 density

text_summary: Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) is a readily available, inexpensive pan-cancer prognostic biomarker calculated from routine complete blood count with differential. NLR reflects the balance between pro-tumor neutrophil-mediated inflammation and anti-tumor lymphocyte-mediated immune surveillance. Elevated NLR (typically >3-5, though optimal cutoffs vary by cancer type and setting) is consistently associated with worse overall survival, progression-free survival, and disease-free survival across virtually all solid tumor types including NSCLC, colorectal, breast, gastric, pancreatic, renal, hepatocellular, and head and neck cancers. In NSCLC treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors, baseline NLR >5 predicts inferior response and survival compared to NLR <=5. Dynamic NLR changes during treatment (decreasing NLR) correlate with treatment response and improved outcomes. NLR is being incorporated into prognostic nomograms and composite risk scores. Limitations include confounding by infection, steroid use, and other inflammatory conditions. Despite extensive validation, NLR has not been incorporated into formal treatment guidelines as a decision-making biomarker but serves as a useful risk stratification tool.
cancer_types: pan-cancer, NSCLC, colorectal, breast, gastric, renal, hepatocellular
predictive_value: Adverse prognosis when elevated; may predict immunotherapy outcomes in NSCLC; treatment response monitoring
testing_method: Complete blood count with differential (automated hematology analyzer)
clinical_cutoff: >3-5 (elevated, unfavorable prognosis; optimal cutoff varies by cancer type and treatment context)

text_summary: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) serum level is a well-established prognostic biomarker in melanoma, lymphoma, and germ cell tumors, reflecting tumor burden, tissue destruction, and metabolic activity. In metastatic melanoma, elevated LDH is an independent adverse prognostic factor incorporated into the AJCC 8th edition staging system, distinguishing M1a-c (normal LDH) from M1d (elevated LDH) subcategories. Patients with elevated LDH have significantly worse overall survival with both immunotherapy and targeted therapy. In the AJCC melanoma staging system, elevated LDH automatically classifies metastatic melanoma as M1c (stage IVM1c). In aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma, LDH is a component of the International Prognostic Index (IPI) and revised IPI (R-IPI) for DLBCL. In germ cell tumors, LDH is part of the International Germ Cell Consensus Classification (IGCCC) risk stratification along with AFP and beta-hCG. LDH reflects the Warburg effect (increased glycolysis) and hypoxia in tumors. Serial LDH monitoring during treatment provides treatment response assessment. Normal LDH values are typically 120-246 U/L but vary by laboratory reference ranges.
cancer_types: melanoma, lymphoma, germ cell tumors, renal, NSCLC
predictive_value: Adverse prognosis when elevated; staging component in melanoma; IPI component in DLBCL; IGCCC component in germ cell tumors
testing_method: Serum enzymatic assay (automated chemistry analyzer)
clinical_cutoff: >upper limit of normal (ULN, typically >246 U/L; varies by laboratory)

text_summary: Beta-2 microglobulin (B2M) is a light chain component of the major histocompatibility complex class I (MHC-I) molecule, shed into serum proportionally to tumor burden in lymphoproliferative disorders. Elevated serum B2M is one of the most powerful prognostic biomarkers in multiple myeloma, forming the basis of the International Staging System (ISS): ISS-I (B2M <3.5 mg/L, median OS ~62 months), ISS-II (B2M 3.5-5.5 mg/L, median OS ~44 months), ISS-III (B2M >=5.5 mg/L, median OS ~29 months). The Revised ISS (R-ISS) integrates B2M with LDH and high-risk cytogenetics (del(17p), t(4;14), t(14;16)) for improved prognostic stratification. In follicular lymphoma, B2M is a component of the FLIPI-2 prognostic index. In CLL, elevated B2M independently predicts shorter time to first treatment and overall survival. B2M also reflects renal function, as it is freely filtered by the glomerulus, necessitating correction in patients with renal impairment. Serial B2M monitoring during myeloma treatment correlates with response depth.
cancer_types: multiple myeloma, lymphoma (follicular, CLL, DLBCL), Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia
predictive_value: ISS staging in multiple myeloma; FLIPI-2 component in follicular lymphoma; CLL prognosis
testing_method: Serum immunoassay (nephelometry, turbidimetry, ECLIA)
clinical_cutoff: ISS: <3.5 mg/L (I), 3.5-5.5 mg/L (II), >=5.5 mg/L (III); normal <2.5 mg/L

text_summary: CA 19-9 (carbohydrate antigen 19-9, sialyl Lewis-a antigen) is the most widely used serum biomarker in pancreatic adenocarcinoma for diagnosis, prognosis, treatment response monitoring, and surveillance. CA 19-9 is elevated (>37 U/mL) in approximately 80% of pancreatic cancers, with levels correlating with tumor burden, resectability, and prognosis. Markedly elevated CA 19-9 (>1000 U/mL) suggests unresectable or metastatic disease. Preoperative CA 19-9 level is an independent prognostic factor: patients with CA 19-9 <200 U/mL after neoadjuvant therapy have better surgical outcomes. Normalization of CA 19-9 after surgery predicts favorable disease-free survival. Serial CA 19-9 monitoring during chemotherapy detects treatment response or progression, often preceding radiographic changes by weeks. Approximately 5-10% of the population are Lewis antigen-negative (Le(a-b-)) and cannot produce CA 19-9 regardless of disease status, representing a key limitation. CA 19-9 is also elevated in cholangiocarcinoma, gastric cancer, and benign biliary obstruction. NCCN recommends CA 19-9 testing at diagnosis, after neoadjuvant therapy, post-resection, and during surveillance every 3-6 months.
predictive_value: Treatment response monitoring; prognosis; resectability assessment in pancreatic cancer
testing_method: Serum immunoassay (ECLIA, CLIA)
clinical_cutoff: >37 U/mL (elevated); >1000 U/mL suggests advanced/unresectable disease; Lewis antigen-negative patients cannot produce CA 19-9

text_summary: HE4 (Human Epididymis Protein 4, WFDC2) is a serum biomarker used in ovarian cancer, particularly as a complement to CA-125 for distinguishing malignant from benign pelvic masses and for monitoring treatment response in epithelial ovarian cancer. HE4 is a small secretory protein of the whey acidic protein (WAP) family with anti-protease activity. The Risk of Ovarian Malignancy Algorithm (ROMA) combines HE4, CA-125, and menopausal status into a composite score that stratifies patients into high and low risk for ovarian malignancy, with sensitivity of 89-94% and specificity of 75-80% for epithelial ovarian cancer. HE4 has higher specificity than CA-125 because it is less frequently elevated in benign gynecologic conditions such as endometriosis. Elevated HE4 at diagnosis is an independent adverse prognostic factor. Serial HE4 monitoring during chemotherapy correlates with treatment response, and rising HE4 during surveillance may detect recurrence. HE4 is elevated in some non-ovarian malignancies (endometrial, lung) and in renal impairment. Normal HE4 reference ranges are age-dependent and menopausal status-dependent, with premenopausal women having lower upper limits of normal (typically <70 pmol/L premenopausal, <140 pmol/L postmenopausal).
predictive_value: Distinguishes malignant from benign pelvic masses; treatment response monitoring; ROMA score component
testing_method: Serum immunoassay (ECLIA, CLIA); ROMA algorithm (HE4 + CA-125 + menopausal status)
clinical_cutoff: <70 pmol/L premenopausal, <140 pmol/L postmenopausal (reference ranges vary); ROMA: high-risk cutoff >=11.4% premenopausal, >=29.9% postmenopausal

text_summary: The Oncotype DX Recurrence Score (RS) is a 21-gene RT-qPCR assay performed on FFPE tumor tissue that generates a continuous score from 0 to 100, quantifying the 10-year risk of distant recurrence and predicting chemotherapy benefit in HR-positive/HER2-negative early-stage breast cancer. The assay measures expression of 16 cancer-related genes (including proliferation genes: Ki-67, STK15, survivin, cyclin B1, MYBL2; invasion genes: MMP11, CTSL2; HER2 group: GRB7, HER2; estrogen group: ER, PGR, BCL2, SCUBE2) and 5 reference genes. The landmark TAILORx trial (10,273 patients) established that RS 0-25 patients can safely forgo chemotherapy, sparing approximately 70% of eligible patients from unnecessary treatment. The RxPONDER trial extended these findings to 1-3 node-positive disease, showing postmenopausal women with RS <=25 derive no chemotherapy benefit. Premenopausal women with RS 16-25 and 1-3 nodes may still benefit from chemotherapy. The Oncotype DX assay is endorsed by NCCN (category 1), ASCO, and St. Gallen guidelines as a standard-of-care test for adjuvant treatment decision-making in early-stage HR+/HER2- breast cancer.
cancer_types: breast (HR-positive, HER2-negative, early-stage, node-negative and 1-3 node-positive)
predictive_value: Predicts 10-year distant recurrence risk and chemotherapy benefit; guides adjuvant treatment decisions
testing_method: 21-gene RT-qPCR on FFPE tissue (Exact Sciences/Genomic Health)
clinical_cutoff: RS 0-15: low risk; RS 16-25: intermediate (age/node-dependent); RS 26-100: high risk, chemotherapy benefit
